coredata.dc:title: Biomass-derived phosphorous-doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQD): An excellent biocompatible material for in-vitro cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Goswami, Juri
Barman, Hiranmoy
Hazarika, Parasa
Manna, Prasenjit
Devi, Aradhana
Saikia, Lakshi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple hydrothermal process was employed to create phosphorous-doped fluorescent carbon quantum dots (P-CQD) from biomass banana stems. The formation of the P-CQDs and the surface functional group was confirmed through a variety of characterization methods, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), (transmission electron microscopy) TEM, (field emission scanning electron microscopy) FESEM, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and (ultraviolet–visible) UV–vis spectroscopy. The quantum yield of the synthesized P-CQDs was found to be 15 %. The P-CQDs showed excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) emission behaviour possessing a blue fluorescence under UV light. The P-CQDs was utilized for in vitro cytotoxicity effect on Muscle (L6) and Kidney cells (HEK293). The minimum cell viability for the P-CQD was 96.24 %, while the maximum cell viability was approximately 100 % at a concentration of 400 μg/mL. This study suggests that P-CQDs were non-toxic and could be employed for fluorescence-based cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biomass
Banana stem
Hydrothermal method
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of phosphorous doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs)
In vitro cytotoxicity effect of P-CQD

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Director, CSIR-NEIST, Jorhat for giving the permission to carry out some part of our research and Principal, JIST for financial support, LS acknowledges in-house project OLP-2078 for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, (c) particle size distribution of P-CQDs.
(a) SEM image, (b) EDS spectrum of P-CQDs.
(a) UV–visible spectrum, (b) PL spectra, (c) XRD pattern, (d) FTIR spectrum of P-CQD.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of P-CQDs, (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s (d) P 2p regions of P-CQDs.
(a) pH effect, (b) PL stability as a function of time of P-CQDs.
Cell viability analysis after incubation with muscle (L6) cells or kidney cells (HEK293) for 24 h using P-CQD at concentrations ranging from 50 to 400 (µg/mL).

Fluorescent images of P-CQDs showing blue fluorescent in muscle cells (L6 cells) or Kidney cells (HEK293). Cells were treated with P-CQD at concentration of 50, 100, and 200 µg/mL. The blue fluorescence indicates the intracellular accumulation of P-CQD. Scale bar 50 µm.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of the P-CQDs and their application for cell imaging.

head.ce:title: Biomass-derived phosphorous-doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQD): An excellent biocompatible material for in-vitro cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Materials Group, Materials Sciences and Technology Division, CSIR- North-East Institute of Science and Technology, Jorhat 785006, Assam, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple hydrothermal process was employed to create phosphorous-doped fluorescent carbon quantum dots (P-CQD) from biomass banana stems. The formation of the P-CQDs and the surface functional group was confirmed through a variety of characterization methods, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), (transmission electron microscopy) TEM, (field emission scanning electron microscopy) FESEM, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and (ultraviolet–visible) UV–vis spectroscopy. The quantum yield of the synthesized P-CQDs was found to be 15 %. The P-CQDs showed excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) emission behaviour possessing a blue fluorescence under UV light. The P-CQDs was utilized for in vitro cytotoxicity effect on Muscle (L6) and Kidney cells (HEK293). The minimum cell viability for the P-CQD was 96.24 %, while the maximum cell viability was approximately 100 % at a concentration of 400 μg/mL. This study suggests that P-CQDs were non-toxic and could be employed for fluorescence-based cell imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, researchers have been concentrating on exploiting biowastes to create carbon-based products including carbon dots [1–3]. Carbon quantum dots (CQD) have a wide range of uses due to their unique chemical and physical features, which include good fluorescence, high water solubility, biocompatibility, and nontoxicity [4–7]. CQDs are widely used as sensor, bio imaging, drug delivery, solar cells, catalysis, photocatalysis etc [8–13]. A number of methods for CQD synthesis, including high temperature pyrolysis [14], hydrothermal [15], microwave heating [16], laser ablation [17], arc discharge [18], electrochemical [19], plasma gas treatment [20], and oxidation by acid [21] have been reported. Among these, the hydrothermal synthesis technique has been regarded as a green sustainable strategy due to its easy synthesis procedure, use of an aqueous medium, and the CQDs obtained have higher quantum yield.

In order to generate biomass-derived CQD, a variety of biomass materials, including Catharanthus roseus [22], water hyacinth [23], mango peel [24], mustard seeds [25], orange juice [26], lemon [27], sugarcane basses [28] etc were used as renewable natural precursors. Because of its superior photoluminescence properties, biomass-derived CQD has garnered immense attention from researchers as a potential replacement for semiconductor quantum dots and other luminescent molecules [29–31].

Doping of CQDs with heteroatoms has recently received a lot of attention [32–35]. The chemical nature of the doped heteroatom influences electron distribution inside the CDs, changing the bandgap energy and thereby increasing CD photoluminescence. Phosphorous-doping is one of the heteroatoms that is investigated in great detail due to its special characteristics [36,37]. In addition to having strong fluorescence (PL) and a high quantum yield, phosphorous-doping CQDs (P-CQDs) had exceptional optical characteristics. Doping atoms in the carbon structure brings localised states that can capture photo-excited electrons and give rise to novel PL features including red shift of luminescence.

Herein, we proposed a green strategy of preparing carbon dots doped with phosphorus via phosphoric acid activation and hydrothermal treatment of biomass banana stem. While the fruit of the banana plant is the most commonly consumed part, the stem also has some interesting properties. Overall, understanding the composition of banana stems can provide insights into their potential uses beyond just being left to rot in plantations. Banana stems contain 38.03 % carbon, 0.42 % nitrogen, and 5.46 % hydrogen. The high carbon content suggests that banana stems could potentially be utilized as a source for synthesizing value-added materials, especially carbon quantum dots. The banana stem, characterized by its lignocellulosic composition comprising hemicellulose (16–20 %), cellulose (43–50 %), and lignin (12–16 %), has emerged as a significant precursor for initiating the production of CQD. Therefore, phosphoric acid is a suitable activation agent since it triggers the cleavage of aryl ether linkages of lignin groups thereby assisting dehydration processes that propel this chemical activation [36].

Currently, carbon quantum dots serve as common fluorescent probes in optical imaging and hold considerable importance for clinical diagnosis [38,39]. The exceptional purity and non-toxic nature of these fabricated materials enable their successful integration into highly sensitive systems, including human cells and animals [40]. Herein, the muscle cell line (L6) line and kidney cell line (HEK293) were chosen to assess the effectiveness of the prepared CQDs. Investigations into the photophysical properties of CDs have shown that they can serve as fluorescent probes for bioimaging applications [41,42]. As a result, the P-CQDs were effective probes for in-vitro cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phosphorous doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A simple hydrothermal method was adopted to synthesize P-CQDs from biomass banana stem. At first, the well dried biomass was grounded well and sieved to get a fine powder. Then certain amount of the dried powders was soaked in H3PO4/H2O medium for 10 h. The reaction was then conducted for 6 h at 180 °C in a Teflon-coated autoclave that continued to operate in a hot air oven. The resultant crude material was then filtered through a 0.22 m membrane to remove larger particles, then underwent 12 h of dialysis in a dialysis bag (1000 MWCO). A yellow colour solution containing P-CQDs was attained after dialysis. For later usage, the synthesised P-CQDs were stored in a refrigerator at 10 C. Furthermore, the as-synthesized P-CQDs have been utilized for in-vitro cell imaging (Scheme 1 ).

The quantum yield (QY) of the as synthesized P-CQDs was calculated using the equation given below:
(1)
∅
CDs
=
∅ R
×
F
F R
×
A R
A
×
È
2
È
R
2
where P-CQDs and R represents the carbon quantum dots and reference (Quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4) respectively. The parameters A, F, Φ and η represents the absorbance values, fluorescence integrated intensities, quantum yield (ØR = 0.54 for Quinine sulphate), and the refractive index (ƞ) of both P-CQDs and quinine sulphate is 1.33.

Both muscle cells (L6) and kidney cells (HEK293) were obtained from Sigma, and their maintenance was carried out by using DMEM (GIBCO) medium. The culture medium (DMEM) was enriched with 10 % FBS (GIBCO), 1 % penicillin, and 1 % streptomycin stock solutions. The cells were incubated in a humidified environment at 37 °C temperature and 5 % CO2. To promote cell growth, a standard T25 flask was utilized until the cells reached 80–90 % confluency. The culture medium was refreshed every other day, and prior to reaching confluency, the cells were trypsinized. For experimental discs or plates, the cells were seeded at a concentration of 1 × 105.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity effect of P-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity assay was carried out by using the Alamar Blue reduction bioassay (Alamar Biosciences, Sacramento, CA). The ability of live cells to reduce Alamar Blue solution is a measure of cellular fitness. Cells withapproximately80 %confluencywere treated with carbon quantum dots (P-CQD) at concentrations of 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, and 400 µg/mL for 24 hrs. After P-CQD treatment, the media was withdrawn and the cells were washed with PBS (pH 7.4). The cells were then kept at incubation for 4 h at 37 °C with 200 µL of Alamar Blue solution. Using a microplate reader, the optical density of each experimental well was determinedat 570 and 600 nm. Cells that received no treatment serving as a control. The outcomes have been assigned as a percentage of the control group. The same study was carried out in quintuplicate.

Muscle (L6) and kidney cells (HEK293) were introduced into a four-chambered culture slide at a concentration of 5 × 105 and allowed to culture for 24 h. Upon reaching approximately 70 % confluency, the cells underwent treatment with varying concentrations of P-CQD (50, 100, and 200 µg/mL) and were further incubated for an additional 24 h. After the treatment, the culture medium was aspirated, and the cells were washed thrice with PBS (pH 7.4). Following this, the cells were fixed with a 4 % paraformaldehyde solution for 30 min at room temperature, followed by three rinses with PBS. Nuclei were then stained using NucRed™ Live 647 ReadyProbes Reagent (ThermoFisher Scientific) according to the provided protocol, and the slide was sealed with a cover slip using Fluoroshield™ histology mounting medium. The slide was kept in a dark and dry environment overnight for optimal imaging. On the subsequent day, cell imaging was carried out using the EVOS M7000 Imaging System fluorescence microscope. Images were captured utilizing a 20× objective lens. Scale bar is 50 µm.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) study was employed to determine the particle morphology and size of P-CQDs. The TEM image (Fig. 1 a, b) shows the formation of carbon quantum dots with irregular spherical type structure with uniform distribution. The lattice distance was measured from the HRTEM image and was found to be 0.21 nm which resembles to the (100) plane of graphite (Fig. 1c). Fig. 1d displays a histogram of the carbon quantum dots sizes, and it shows that the average size lies between 8 and 10 nm.

Fig. 2 a represents the FE-SEM analysis of phosphoric acid-soaked dried banana stem powders. The SEM image shows the tiny distinct particles with irregular and coarse surfaces. The accompanying SEM-EDS images (Fig. 2b) of the carbonised biomass samples show that they are mostly made up of components like C, O, P, and so on. The corresponding weight % and atomic % of carbon, oxygen and phosphorous elements are shown as inset in Fig. 2b.

To investigate the optical characteristics of the P-CQDs, the UV–Vis and fluorescence emission spectra were obtained. It was observed from Fig. 3 a that after being exposed to UV light, the aqueous solution of P-CQDs emits a bright blue colour (inset in Fig. 3a). The UV–visible absorption band at 260 nm was attributed to the π → π* transition in the (CC) bonds in sp2 domains. The PL (photoluminescence) spectra shows maximum λem value at 450 nm which corresponds to blue emission. Fig. 3b demonstrates the photoluminescence emission spectra of P-CQDs at various wavelengths of excitation with 10 nm increments. In the PL emission spectra of P-CQDs, there occurs variations in the PL intensities when the excitation wavelength differs from 270 to 360 nm. This demonstrates that, in contrast to common semiconductor quantum dots, the PL emission of P-CQDs is entirely excitation-dependent, which is a fundamental characteristic of CQDs. Moreover, the calculated QY of the as-synthesized P-CQDs was found to be 15 %.

The XRD pattern of the as synthesized P-CQDs have been shown in Fig. 3c. A broad single peak at around 24.7° indicated that the obtained P-CQDs had an amorphous nature. This broad reflection was found to be generated by the graphite (002) interlayer lattice gap and was related to the uniform ordering of carbon atoms and weak crystalline carbons.

The as-synthesized P-CQDs have been characterized by FTIR analysis to recognize the reactive functional groups present on the surface of P-CQDs. As displayed in Fig. 3d, the wide peak at 3423.0 cm−1 and a peak at 2935.4 cm−1 has been assigned to OH and CH2 groups respectively. The three peaks at 607.7, 1137.5 and 2377.6 cm−1 has been attributed to PO4 3−, PO and P-H, thus proving the P element doping in P-CQDs.
Fig. 3(e) depicts the optimal excitation and emission spectra. The maximum emission (λem) of 450 nm was noticed at an excitation wavelength (λex) of 350 nm.

The elemental composition of the P-CQDs was studied using XPS analysis. Fig. 4 a depicts the XPS survey spectrum with peak positions at 286.2, 500.3 and 134.5 eV that has been assigned to C (carbon), O (oxygen) and P (phosphorous) elements respectively. Fig. 4b displays the C1s deconvoluted XPS spectra which shows a peak at 286.6 for CO bonds and a peak at 283.3 eV that has been allocated to and C–C/CC bonds. Fig. 4c shows the O1s deconvoluted spectra at 531.0 that has been assigned to CO bond. The deconvoluted XPS spectra of P2p shows binary peaks at 133.6 and 134.2 eV which were ascribed to P-O and P-C bonds respectively (Fig. 4d). Thus, the XPS analysis confirms the presence of carbon, oxygen and phosphorous as elemental composition on P-CQDs with subsequent bond formation of P with C and O elements.

The photoluminescence properties of the as-prepared CDs under different pH systems and time duration have been studied. Fig. 5 a depicts the pH effect on the photoluminescence properties of P-CQDs. The pH of the reaction medium was found to be mildly acidic under normal circumstances, but HCl and Na2CO3 were used to adjust it from 2 to 12. It was observed that the PL intensities were weak at highly acidic and basic solutions. Moreover, maximum PL intensities were observed at pH 7. For any fluorometric probe, the stability of carbon dots is a crucial consideration. It was noted that even after 100 days, the PL intensity remained constant (Fig. 5b).

The highest fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 7, likely because this pH represents a balanced state where neither excessive acidity nor excessive alkalinity interferes significantly with the fluorescence properties of the CQDs. At extremely low and high pH conditions, PL intensities decrease due to the destruction of emissive sites on the particles under severe pH conditions. Under strong acidic and basic conditions, protonation and deprotonation occur which results in decreasing electron transition between π-π* states, hence resulting in decreased PL intensities.

For any fluorometric probe, the stability of carbon dots is a crucial consideration. It was noted that even after 100 days, the PL intensity remained constant (Fig. 5b). The effect of salt ions (NaCl) was studied to study the stability of carbon dots. The effect of salt ions (NaCl) on the PL intensity of CQDs was performed to study the stability of carbon dots. Fig. 5c shows that the PL intensity of carbon dots was stable under different ionic strengths.

The assessment of the biocompatibility of P-CQD is crucial as the quantum dots might come in contact with cells. In order to determine the toxicity levels of P-CQD for potential future uses, an in vitro cell viability experiment was carried out. P-CQD was incubated with either muscle cells or kidney cells at concentrations of 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, and 400 µg/mL for 24 h (Fig. 6 ). The results of the cellular toxicity test revealed that the cell viability was unaffected by the various P-CQD dosages. The minimum cell viability for the P-CQD was 96.24 %, while the maximum cell viability was approximately 100 % at a concentration of 400 μg/mL. The findings demonstrated that P-CQD treatment were non-toxic on both muscle and kidney cells and hence can be used for biomedical application.

Fluorescent imaging was performed to examine the application of P-CQD for the bioimaging study. Threedifferent dosages ofP-CQDwere chosen to assess the cellular internalization. Fig. 7 illustrates that P-CQD have a blue fluorescence, implying that quantum dots are localised inside thecells. When the treatment dosageswere raised, cellular internalisation increased progressively. Fig. 7 also shows that muscle and kidney cells exhibit normal cell morphology following theaddition of P-CQD. This study suggests that P-CQD were non-toxic and could be employed for fluorescence-based imaging of biological samples.

This work effectively established a simple and eco-friendly method for synthesizing P-CQDs using the banana stem as a natural precursor. The as-prepared P-CQDs emit a bright blue photoluminescence under UV light. Moreover, the P-CQDs display excellent water solubility and stability with a high QY of 15 %. Furthermore, the as-obtained P-CQDs had an uneven spherical structure with 9 nm average size. Additionally, the P-CQDs possess less cytotoxicity with excellent biocompatibility and were applied for in-vitro cell imaging. Thus, the prepared P-CQDs may be used as an eco-friendly fluorescent nanomaterial for future application in biomedical applications.

Juri Goswami: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hiranmoy Barman: Methodology, Data curation. Parasa Hazarika: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis. Prasenjit Manna: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Aradhana Devi: Methodology. Lakshi Saikia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Director, CSIR-NEIST, Jorhat for giving the permission to carry out some part of our research and Principal, JIST for financial support, LS acknowledges in-house project OLP-2078 for financial support.
